text: can make a monitor company. There are too many of them. Maybe the monitor company itself shuts down. Maybe it's an Indian monitoring, monitor company, and it shuts down. But let's say the finance company, the worst case is they get into some fraud. But does it affect you? No. You might not be implicated in the fraud, but you lose your job, right? That's the worst case. What's the tail risk? Tail risk is something where, you know, I really recommend people read Taleb. There's an entire book about antifragile that Taleb has written, uh, which is about... And there's a lot of information on it about tail risk, which is what are the risks when you're not calculating? Like stuff like pandemic, right? What is this, this extra risk That you just never-- like maybe your health goes bad. Right?

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: A lot of people in their twenties, they like overspending, but like twenty-seven, twenty-eight, almost reliably you get a health issue. Almost everyone will get it, right? Maybe in their thirties they get it. Like maybe you're part of the thirty percent that don't get it. But like still some people will get some, some health related, back pain, this that, whatever. Small, small things. How-- like can you factor those in as well? Right? So two years later, knowing where you will be and what will happen to you is so challenging because the world is so ambiguous, right? Like you don't know what's gonna happen to you in two years. So you model all of these out, you write them next to each other on a piece of paper, and then you feed them into ChatGPT and say, "What do you think is the more probabilistic outcome here?" And you ask it to do deep research. So it actually does research on the

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: market and gives you likelihood for out of the hundred finance com-- because AI can help you do this. Out of the hundred finance companies that were started last year in India, how many shut down because of A, B, C, D reasons? How many of their analysts their, their salary went up? You look at all the monetary companies. ChatGPT can do full deep research and give you this. And it can give you a score and can tell you why. Humans cannot do it because you have to spend so much time doing research. None of you do that much research on your careers, especially people wa-watching this, right? Or your industry that you're in. It's just like [speaking native language] But it's like you can actually model the risks between multiple different companies with AI, with deep research right now. Okay? Think long term, two years, plus worry about incentives of everyone else participating. So not only

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: have you thought about think long term, what is the incentives about everybody else participating? Like a good example of this is freelancing, right? Like, and I've spoken multiple times about freelancing, but I'll tell you one problem with freelancing, which nobody's, nobody's kind of talking about on the internet, which is that why does a company hire a freelancer? Why do we hire freelancers? Why does any company hire freelancers? Because you don't want to take the risk of hiring someone full-time. Because why? You are not sure this is a full-time project. Right. That's why companies hire freelancers. You don't want to hire somebody full-time. So like, let me hire somebody part-time. Or it's a small company. So like, you know, some founder starting something new and they just don't have the cash flow. They don't even know in three months if they're gonna be alive. So when you

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: freelance, and especially when you freelance with small startups, and there's a reason, by the way, Aosh doesn't work with small startups. It's not because I don't like working with small startups. It's simply because I don't know if they're gonna be around in three months or six months. I don't know how much financial instability they have. Whereas when we work with large companies, for example, we work with Coca-Cola, right? You and I both know Coca-Cola is gonna exist next year. Maybe there's a one percent chance something happens and they die, or zero-point something percent chance, but we can put that in tail risk. We can reliably say Coca-Cola is gonna be around, around next year. So I'd rather work with them than some small startup where I know ninety-nine percent of those small startups will die next year. So tell me then, why do all of you freelance with small startups? Why does

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: anyone freelance with small startups? When I won't take that risk because I've modeled it, why aren't you modeling that? Because the obvious point is, well, that's the only startups that will work with me. That's the only people I know, so I'm gonna do some work with them. But then know what you're getting into. The two-year-out outcome there is that startup dies. So unless you can diversify out of that startup and work with five, ten, fifteen other... ideally get to the bigger companies quickly, you're playing in this-- you're-- the-- maybe you're making money, but the risk you're absorbing is huge. You're actually burning years from your career because maybe in three years, all the companies you worked with will have died. And then worry about, like I said, worry about o-other people's incentives. A person, a founder takes on freelancers when they're not sure about the role or when

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: they don't have enough money. And to be honest, their incentive here matters because if you don't matter to them, like why do you expect them to take care of you if things get hard, right? Okay? The next thing about risks is to beware irreversible consequences. Um, which is that there's this-- like sometimes there could be this irreversible consequence that when you leave a company, you burn bridges with the company. When you leave a company, you're like, "I'm not talking to this person. They're horrible people," and you go badmouth the company, right? We see this a lot in, in general in India. But that's an irreversible consequence, 'cause after that, the company is not gonna work in any form or fashion. So the company gets big, then you're in trouble because you have locked off a particular section of capital flow into you, which is the company and their, all their related, you know,

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: customers, this, that, whatever, right? That ecosystem you locked yourself out of. So one thing we have learnt in India is never burn bridges. Even me, right? Like when I, when I was twenty or whatever, I would burn some bridges where I'd be like, "Who cares? I'm not gonna work with this person again." But now that I'm in my thirties, even if someone's wronged us, we tend to maintain good relationships, right? Because we're like, we never know where this person can go, and maybe this person can mature and end up building a big company. So we try our best to maintain good relationships even if-- when people are leaving, right? But also tail risks are overestimated. Like sometimes when in planning, we can also say pandemic might be there, this might be there, that might be there. Sometimes they're overestimated, right? Some of you might not do something where you're like, "What if this

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: small percentage chance of thing happens?" But most likely it might not happen. Okay. Remember we spoke about optionality one or two cuttas prior to this? One of the fake optionality things is I will interview at ten places just to know whether they want me. Here, this is fake optionality. Why is it fake optionality? Because you haven't quit yet, right? You don't even know if these are serious offers, whether you'll survive with the company for six months. There's so many things that happen after a company is saying yes to you in a, in an interview, right? But some people interview just to [muffled speaking] What other people do, like doing this is fundamentally a waste of time because you're not quitting, you're not planning to join any of those companies. But real optionality is instead of constantly validating myself, going to ten companies and knowing, yeah, yeah, you know, market

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: still wants me, what if you build a set of skills that's completely transferable? The question is, and the reason life is so different from poker or any of these games is you can build a set of skills that works no matter whereYou can be anti-fragile. You can be the kind of person that can absorb any sort of risk. Of course, there are risks, like you get hit by a car, you can't absorb that, right? But you can be very careful and say, "I won't even go out on the street." Right? But at the same time, you can build a personality that makes sure that no risk really affects you. I'll tell you how. It's called something called deep optionality. I found this on Hans Rollins Substack. I was reading it some moons ago, right? In the way of a master martial artist, a system endowed with deep optionality reacts to potentially disruptive events with a response tailored to the problem at hand. While

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: this property is surely desirable for every aspect of system design, it's impracticable to implement deep optionality in any but the few everyday challenges of your system. I'll give you a very good example of deep optionality. Do you know humans can walk on any surface? Almost all surfaces you can walk on. We spend a lot of time practicing this, walking. You don't even think about it. You can walk on this. You can balance. You can walk on like, like, you know, like, like beanbag surfaces. You can walk on mud. You even know how to hop on like hot surfaces if it's very, very hot. You have deep optionality with which surface you can walk on. My question is, why don't you have this with your careers or anything that you're taking risks with? Why don't you have this deep level of optionality? And no, it doesn't come from interviewing in ten places. It comes from being absolutely

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: indispensable, and it comes through repetition and master-mastery. The number of times you have learned to walk and fallen and walk and fallen, walk and fallen, walk and fallen. The number of times I've run a company, shut down, run a company, failed, run a company, acquired, run a company, small outcome. So many times I've done it that today, no matter what the surface is, whether I'm running cohorts, whether I'm running services companies, whether I'm running game dev, I can walk on any surface. And everybody can learn to do it. What are those skills? What is the skill set you need to play in any surface when it comes to life, when it comes to careers, when it comes to business, is something that is deeply acquirable. And it boils down to a bunch of things, right? It boils down to be able to, you know, take risk accurately, learn this entire chapter. It boils down to hiring good

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: people, getting good people around you. Okay? It hires... It boils down to being wealth sharing, not wealth taking. Because if you're taking risk and you don't want to spend money, you have to give equity around, right? You have to give your co-founders equity. So it boils down to how well you structure those. It boils down a lot to distribution. Okay? The amount of people who know you, the amount of, you know, views you can get on a specific thing significantly lowers your risk. You can try five apps, ten apps, ten products, ten services until you find the one that works. And you only have to be right once. The cool thing about life, the difference between, let's say, a game like poker and life is in poker, if you go all in, you win something, you go all in the next time you might get bust. But in life, if you go all in, even if you are right once, it's enough. It'll pay for all your

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: thousands of experiments, as long as you're not foolish and spend all your money on it, which nobody does with their real money. Okay? So ultimately, you have to become the kind of person that can stand any risk. And to do that, you have to learn these things like l-learning to take risk, learning to talk, learning to put people together. And trust me, hard skills are not part of this. Okay? Now, hard skills can be temporarily part of this, which is I'm a very good video editor. Any company right now in the next few years will hire me. But what about ten, twenty years later when video editing becomes less valuable? Right? So you still need to learn the other stuff. But some people say, "I don't need that stuff today. I'm a great engineer." Like three years ago, you know, all, all of us, including me as an engineer, were like, "Yeah, I'm invulnerable. I'm an engineer. I'm always gonna get

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: paid really well." And then the rug was pulled under our feet, right, by new technology. So I would say the timeless things that humans have always done ha-- is something that you need to get good grip and mastery over. Okay? Howard Marks has a good way of talking about this. He calls this second-level thinking. He says the real danger isn't what everyone fears, it's what everybody ignores. Uh, so for example, if everyone's panicking, risk is actually lower. If everyone's very scared about something [laughing] Okay? You must take risks to win, but taking risks guarantees nothing. So before you take a risk, build shock absorbers, which is cash, broad skills. Anyone who wants to freelance, anyone who wants to start their own company, make sure, in my opinion, have at least ten lakhs in your bank. It's a good number for India because ten lakhs grants you at least if you're living very

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: cheaply. I remember when I was doing Jobspire, I was living on like some fifteen thousand rupees a month. There were three of us. We were in Delhi. We had this small, you know, three BHK apartment, which is like, you know, three of us were sharing, so it's... And we were in some dungeon, right? So it's like costs were low.We had a little bit of funding. We were barely using it. We were using, like, the funding like some, you know, FD money or something like that. We were running the business on it. That's how frugal we were. Sometimes when I see these startup founders spending money, I just get scared. I'm just like, "I've never run a company like this." Even when I was funded, I never ran a company like that. But you build shock absorbers, okay? So I know that I could have taken that risk because I always had ten lakhs in my bank from my freelancing days, knowing that worst-case

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: scenario, I at least have a year where I'll be fine. It is suicidal to take risks without having shock absorption, okay? And I'll tell you one thing, and I'm doing a video-- I'm doing another cutdown on this, maybe a few, a few cutdowns later, which is about how wealthy people think. It's very important to study how wealthy people think. Wealthy people, the difference between you and wealthy people is they have a totally different risk, risk profile than you. A wealthy person doesn't mind spending money on reputation and power, whereas for the average person, the only lever you have is wealth. Like, I have X money, this other person has X money, you're only comparing that. But wealthy people compare reputation and power a lot, and they spend a lot on it. And they can spend on it because they have enough of a bank reserve to take more and more risks. So we look at, you know, these

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: entrepreneurs who are serial entrepreneurs, who start their second business, third business, fourth business, fifth business. We're like, "Wow, crazy. Five businesses." But have you thought of the fact that they're able to do that because their first business probably worked or their second business probably worked? And now they have the cash balance, even if they go to zero, they'll be fine. Even if the third business doesn't work out. Like, the Twitch founder is a good example of this, Justin Kan. He started Twitch, did well, made money, got an exit, then started a legal startup which completely failed. But did it affect him personally? Absolutely not. Maybe reputation-wise, yes, but cash-wise, no chance. Because cash-wise, the person has made enough and more money. So it's not really a risk. Like Unleashed Avatar is a very good example, right? Unleashed Avatar is something where even

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: if it doesn't work out, personally, it's not really gonna affect me. Or rather, company-wise, it's not gonna affect the company. I've built it in such a way where it's like it doesn't kill EOS on its way out if it doesn't work, right? And these are the type of risks you're supposed to take. And there are-- Like, for example, if I did a robotics startup right now, it would definitely kill EOS if it doesn't work. 'Cause the amount of CapEx, capital expenditure required to make a robotics startup work, it will definitely kill EOS. Just you need, like, hundreds of millions of dollars to make it work. So maybe someday, once I have enough shock absorption, once I have enough cash in the bank. Like somebody was telling me, right? L&T or something, one of those companies has, like,

They can take any risks they want. But yet they do not take risks because for them, the risks are reputational now. They're a publicly traded company. If they go out and build some app and it doesn't work or build some AI thing and it doesn't work, the market will-- all the stocks will dip. I want to tell you three stories to show you how risk and reward really works and how random life is. Okay? I was, I was on a plane, I was flying, I was flying to Bombay or something like that, and I met a guy, he was sitting next to me. Uh, and cool thing is like in business class, everybody's very chatty. Everyone talks a lot. So I was speaking to this guy, and it was just like generally chatting, and he knew about what we were

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: doing. Um, and I asked him: What do you do? He's like: No, I, I sell furniture. So he's running a franchisee of a furniture store in India. And I was like: How big is the business? It's a small number. Not a very big business. Then we were just chatting, and I was just like, you know, he looked like he was forty or something. Forty-five, fifty or something like that. I was like, "So what is your net worth? How are you flying business class and all with this kind of revenue?" It's not a very high profit business either. And he's like: "Actually, it's funny. Um, my net worth is in the hundreds of crores." I was like: "Wait, what? But the business math doesn't make sense." He's like, "No, no, no. When I was like twenty-three, me and my friends bought a property." Some random property they bought, and then he's like, "Something happened, and the government really needed it for something, and

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: they bought it out for, whatever, some few hundred crores." He said, "That time property was cheap. We didn't know what we were doing. We just bought it. And then now the government want-wanted to buy it like a few years ago for like lots of money. And I made all my money there." And he's like, "I don't have kids, so I'm just spending the money now." It's crazy. The guy runs a business-- I mean, the guy runs a furniture business, but his source of wealth is not furniture. His source of wealth is some random, you know, gamble that he's taken on the side that has ended up working very well. And this, by the way, is a story of many people's lives. It's just like... But you still need to take those gambles. You still need to build the unleash the avatars. You still need to build something. You still need to keep trying like five, ten percent of your portfolio on crazy risk. And maybe it

text: works out, right? And he bought property why? Because he found it interesting, right? Him and his friends had a cool plan and they said, "Chalo karenge." Most of the successful businesses you see in life are just three friends with a cool plan saying chalo karenge. It's never like a planned one, two, three, four, five unless they have a lot of experience in that domain. Like EOS is a plan. You've seen the Disney-- Some of you have seen the Disney plan that we had, right? Disney-like plan where we build, you know, talent and the talent features. It worked according to plan, but that's rare. And even I accept that what we have done is fairly rare in terms of sticking according to plan. Even then, it's not a hundred percent according to plan. videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: ten percent random risks. Okay? And remember we've spoken about diversification versus, um, confusion, which is diversification is you still need one thing that works, and you need to still put your money in other things that don't require your time. Real estate didn't require his time. His time goes into the furniture business, right? So you need to find outlets like that where you can invest either your money, whether it could be risky, it could be non-risky, up to you, but model that portfolio correctly. text: finally, the guy bought it because he's like Narayan Murthy is a friend, I'll buy it. Today, the value of his shares is massive. His next five generations run on this. Like, there'll be talent here and, and I would have told them, "Yeah, I will do this. It might work," whatever, against their best wishes. Some people just be like, "Yeah, I would rather have a, you know, ten thousand rupees increase in my salary this month." But there's some people I went to and I was like, "Why-- Instead of taking that, why don't you take a risk and do this?" And they did it, and then the outcome surprises them. And it's like a permanent elevation in like mind space, 'cause they can't relate to the person that didn't want to take

text: risk or have some exposure to risk. They can't relate to that person anymore. But a lot of you are like that where like you haven't even modeled how your risk profile is going to be outside of the primary salary that you get. So yeah, Infosys shares, again, another example of random stuff. By the way, do you know how some venture funded founders in India make money? People who have raised a lot of money, how they make money? Some of these guys take reasonably good salaries for what the market should be paying them, and then what they do is they invest in their friends' companies. Perfectly legal. You're allowed to do this. And they all kind of invest in each other's companies. So there are a hundred founders, they all invest in each other's companies. And the reason to do this is when you invest in the very early stages, it's either hundred x or bust. Okay? It's either going hundred x or

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: bust. So a lot of founders in India right now who have invested in so many startups now, those startups have done very, very well, that those-- that their investment portfolio itself is much more valuable than their companies. It's crazy, right? And it's not a bad thing or a wrong thing. In fact, this is-- in Silicon Valley, this is a known, a known thing, and it's necessary to do also because you don't know if your company is going to work, right? There's so many tail risks. So sometimes I'll meet founders like, "I'm going all on my company. This is what..." I'm like, even the best founders in India know there is a risk that their company might not work, and therefore they do this, right? To hedge themselves from this risk, right? So again, there are founders in India who made more money investing in other companies than their own companies. It's not a bad thing because you don't have

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: complete control of whether your company will work or not. But this offsetting of risk is important, okay? And the reason many people don't take risk is because losses feel bad. Humans-- Have you heard of something called loss aversion? It's a theory where a loss hurts you much higher than a win. If you win, you're likeLike, I'll give you a good example. We invested, uh, fifteen lakhs in a company recently. Uh, we invested this from YAAS and the company's done very well, probably up two x, three x, and we're just like... We're feeling bad about it. I'll tell you why. Because we had the opportunity to invest in this company a year b-prior. Okay? And we're just like, I told you, timeless greed. This, that. Timeless. And I, you know, I was just like: It's okay. We played safe. Yes, we played at the series A, series B level. Outcome is capped, but it's okay. We, you know, sometimes when you

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: make these, these things, the company could have also gone to zero, no? So loss feels bad, more worse than gain because we-- the gain also we felt a little bit bad, but if we had lost money, if we had lost that fifteen lakhs, we'd be like, "Shit, such a bad decision. How bad we are. We ne-we should never do this again." Because I know how loss feels. So one of the things I started doing with my own teams is I don't give them crazy ups if I have to take it down. For example, I don't give people titles and promotions just because they've done a very good job. 'Cause yes, it's enjoyable when you get it, but if you lose it, if I take it away from you later, you'll feel twice as bad than when you-- than the goodness you felt when you got it. So a week in when you get it, you'll feel very good, then you'll forget about it. But if you lose it, you'll curse me for the rest of your life, right?

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: So no feel-good promotions, no ballpark numbers or projections, right? So if, if I put an estimate and I say, "Well, you guys, you're gonna make this much for yourself this year," if I put a s-accurate estimation, man, people always fall short of it, right? And then they feel bad because even though they made much more money than everybody else, they're still like, "But I fell down from my target of what was promised to me," right? It still feels like loss. No perks unless well thought out. The minute you give somebody a perk, smallest of perk. Like, for example, we made shoes. Simple thing which I gave to the best talent in our team. We did a collab with Gully Labs and I gave them th-these shoes, cool shoes. But, like, some people didn't get it. People who it's almost there but, like, not there or maybe my oversight or whatever, right? And they felt really bad 'cause they lost out on

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: it. They didn't get it, right? So loss feels worse. And finally, like I said, lower reward versus expectation feels pretty bad. Okay, so the strategy that you should probably use is to keep eighty to ninety percent ultra safe. Eighty to ninety-- I'll tell you our breakup, right? Eighty to ninety percent of our revenue comes from services, right? Some comes from maybe single digits comes from cohorts. Okay. And we s-we invest and save this pretty well. Very safe. But on ten percent, I'm allowed to go crazy and do and unleash the avatar. Right? And I think this is possible with everybody else's life. Like, if you're investing, you don't have to invest one hundred percent of your money in some new penny stock that's going up. You could try a small amount, test a bit and stage it. 'Cause I know how this feel. Like, you know, there's stories, okay, of, of like people who had invested in

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: something very, very early, some stock very, very early, and it goes up like ten, twenty, thirty x over five years, but they sold it in two years, right? So even when exiting stock, the best investors in India, I'll tell you how venture cap-capitalists or private equity think about this. They'll invest, let's say, let's say I invest a crore in a company, okay? I'm just talking, I'm just giving you some smaller numbers. A crore in a company. After three rounds, let's say a new investor wants to come in. I'll sell my stake to that new investor, but not all of my stake, okay? I'll get back the amount of capital I put in. So let's say that one crore has become four crores. I'll be like: "Listen, I don't wanna sell everything. I wanna sell twenty-five percent. I wanna take one crore back." My original corpus has been returned. Okay? Now three crores of stock I still have, hoping this goes

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: somewhere else. Now let's say that three crores becomes nine crores in another round. Now I'll be like, "Let me take out three crores. Let me still keep six crores worth of stock there," and so on and so forth. Safe way to play it. Because I'll tell you why. If you don't do this, and there are many examples of private equity investors in India who invest in companies, they've been very like, you know, high conviction. Yeah, yeah, I think the company will do very well. They haven't sold at any secondary even though they were given the chance, and then the company dies. 'Cause tail risk. The longer you stay alive, the more likely you get hit by tail risks. So ten years out, something might happen, founder might get tired, something might happen in the... And or, you know, some pandemic might happen, the company might shut down, and then suddenly the investor's like, "Shit, I should have

videoTitle: How To Take Risk Like Rich People Do (1 Hour Masterclass)
text: sold something." So no matter, you know, how good our investm-investments might be in, in some of these companies we, we've made in the last couple of years, we'll still take out our original corpus when we get the opportunity.
